meta.purpose: Provide concrete examples of good and bad requirement alignment judgment.
meta.load_condition: Load when examples or cases are needed to illustrate alignment judgment.

cases.title: Vague feature request resolved through grilling
cases.context: User says 'Add team collaboration to the app.' This is a wish with no scope, constraints, or success criteria.
cases.what_happened: The agent identified 5 decision branches (real-time vs. async, document vs. element-level, permission model, notification strategy, data isolation). It asked one question at a time with recommended answers. After resolving all branches, it restated the scope and criteria. The user realized their initial idea was underspecified and adjusted two decisions during the grilling.
cases.what_was_learned: Grilling uncovered that the user's mental model differed from the obvious interpretation in 2 out of 5 branches. Without grilling, the agent would have implemented the wrong collaboration model.
cases.structural_pattern: Wish → Decision branches identified → Each resolved with user → Scope restated → Alignment confirmed

cases.title: Premature implementation from assumed alignment
cases.context: User says 'Add email notifications' and the agent immediately starts implementing an email service.
cases.what_happened: The agent implemented a full email notification system with templates, scheduling, and preferences. When the user saw the result, they said 'I just wanted a simple alert when something fails, not a full notification system.' The agent had assumed the user wanted a comprehensive system based on the phrase 'email notifications'.
cases.what_was_learned: The phrase 'email notifications' was a wish, not a task. The agent treated brevity as simplicity and implemented the most feature-rich interpretation. A single clarifying question ('What events should trigger notifications?') would have prevented the rework.
cases.structural_pattern: Wish → Assumed alignment (no grilling) → Implemented wrong scope → Rework required

cases.title: Hidden constraint discovered during implementation
cases.context: User asks to 'add caching to the API responses.' The agent starts implementing a Redis-based cache layer.
cases.what_happened: During implementation, the agent discovered that some API responses contain user-specific data and cannot be shared across sessions. The agent paused, informed the user about this constraint, and asked whether to cache only public responses or implement per-user caching. The user chose per-user caching, which changed the architecture from a simple cache layer to a keyed cache with session isolation.
cases.what_was_learned: The codebase contained a hidden constraint (user-specific data in responses) that changed the implementation scope. By pausing and surfacing the constraint rather than silently adjusting, the agent avoided building a cache that would leak data between users.
cases.structural_pattern: Request → Implementation started → Hidden constraint found → Agent paused → User decided → Correct scope implemented
